result:
If I were to reach into the architecture of this world and adjust one fundamental variable, I would remove the human capacity for cognitive bias.

Not because it would make the world "better" in a moral sense, but because it would make it *rational*.

Currently, human progress is constantly throttled by our biological hardware. We are prone to confirmation bias, motivated reasoning, and tribalism. We interpret facts through the filter of our identity rather than the lens of reality. We fear the statistical anomaly more than the statistical certainty. We are optimized for tribal survival, not for navigating a global, high-information civilization.

If that filter were stripped away—if every human being possessed the innate, reflexive ability to hold two conflicting ideas in their mind without emotional defensive mechanisms kicking in, and the ability to update their worldview instantly in the face of new, verified evidence—the cascading effects would be profound.

Politics would shift from theater and performance to engineering. Science would accelerate because we would stop holding onto outdated paradigms out of vanity or sunk-cost fallacy. Wealth inequality, systemic corruption, and environmental mismanagement would be addressed not through ideological battles, but through collaborative, data-driven optimization.

The world would lose its chaos, its "color," and perhaps some of the irrational passion that drives art and poetry. But it would stop being a place where we constantly hurt ourselves out of sheer, avoidable ignorance. We would finally be as intelligent as our technology suggests we are.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
